description: The figure presents a scatter plot correlating aluminum content with mass density reduction in Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steels. The horizontal x-axis displays aluminum content from 0 to 14 wt.%, while the left vertical y-axis shows mass density ranging from 6.50 to 8.00 g/cm³ and the right y-axis indicates corresponding density reduction percentages from 0% to 18%. A reference line at 7.874 g/cm³ denotes conventional steels. Data series include conventional TRIP/TWIP steels (open circle, ~7.30 g/cm³ at ~3% Al) and multiple low-density variants represented by squares of varying sizes: TRIPLEX steels, low-density TWIP steels, and low-density duplex steels. The dataset is further categorized by manganese content into three groups (<18% Mn, 18-26% Mn, and 26-30% Mn). The plot reveals a clear inverse relationship where increasing aluminum content progressively decreases density from conventional levels (~7.87 g/cm³) down to approximately 6.50 g/cm³ at ~13% Al, achieving density reductions up to 18%. This demonstrates that aluminum addition is an effective strategy for developing lightweight austenitic steels while preserving the twinning-induced plasticity (TWIP) effect.
